message.content: Adversaries may exploit the Event Monitor Daemon (emond) to gain persistence and elevate privileges by executing malicious content triggered by specific events. Emond, a Launch Daemon, processes events from various services through a rules engine and takes predefined actions. These rules, stored in plist format, can define events like system startup or user authentication and actions such as running system commands. If adversaries write malicious rules, they can execute commands with root privileges, as emond runs with elevated permissions. Common exploitation methods include creating or modifying rule files to trigger malicious actions during critical events. Mitigation strategies involve monitoring and restricting access to the emond configuration files, ensuring only authorized users can modify them, and employing integrity checks to detect unauthorized changes. Regular audits and employing endpoint detection and response (EDR) solutions can also help identify and prevent such abuses.
